metaDescription: A first-72-hours checklist for employers served with an EEOC charge: deadlines, litigation hold, EPLI notice, retaliation briefing, and position statement strategy.
summary: A 14-item checklist for in-house counsel and HR leaders on what to do in the first 72 hours after receiving an EEOC charge of discrimination, covering deadlines, preservation, insurance notice, and position statement strategy.
keywords: how to respond to an EEOC charge
EEOC charge response checklist for employers
notify EPLI carrier of EEOC charge
who should investigate an EEOC charge

faq.question: What should an employer do first after receiving an EEOC charge?
faq.answer: Read the charge twice before reacting, noting who is named, which bases are checked, and the date range alleged, then calendar every deadline on the notice the same day, including the position-statement due date (often around 30 days) and any mediation election window. The duty to preserve has also attached, so issue a litigation hold right away.

faq.question: Do I need to notify my insurance carrier about an EEOC charge?
faq.answer: Yes. Many employment practices liability (EPL) policies treat an administrative charge as a "claim," and late notice is one of the few ways to lose coverage you already paid for, so loop in your broker the same day.

faq.question: How long do I have to file an EEOC position statement?
faq.answer: The position-statement due date is set on the EEOC's notice and is often around 30 days. Calendar it the same day you receive the charge and set internal working-backward dates; extension requests are common, but never assume one will be granted.

faq.question: Why is retaliation a concern while an EEOC charge is pending?
faq.answer: An adverse action while a charge is pending creates a second claim, and retaliation claims are often stronger in front of a jury than the underlying charge. Scheduling, assignments, and reviews for the charging party should be defensible and documented, and the management chain should be briefed accordingly.

faq.question: Should I write an aggressive EEOC position statement?
faq.answer: No. The EEOC will generally release your position statement to the charging party on request, so write facts, attach documents, and skip the adjectives and overclaiming. A modest, accurate position statement ages far better than a forceful one, because it may later be read next to your witnesses' deposition testimony.

faq.question: Is EEOC mediation worth electing?
faq.answer: Agency mediation is voluntary, early, and cheap relative to what follows, and it is not a sign of weakness; for some charges it is plainly the right business outcome. Take a real position and decide with the business rather than declining by default.
